articles.title: Trump’s Education Department Leaves Mountain of Public Records Requests Unaddressed
articles.summary: Even before massive staff cuts at the Department of Education, it could take years for officials to process a request under the Freedom of Information Act. But now, some offices that handled the hundreds of inquiries that pour in each month — from parents, reporters and watchdog groups — have either been eliminated or have […]
articles.full_body:
Title: Trump’s Education Department Leaves Mountain of Public Records Requests Unaddressed

URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/article/trumps-education-department-leaves-mountain-of-public-records-requests-unaddressed

Published Time: 2026-07-21T06:30:00-04:00

Markdown Content:
_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)

Even before massive staff cuts at the Department of Education, it could take years for officials to process a request under the Freedom of Information Act.

But now, some offices that handled the hundreds of inquiries that pour in each month — from parents, reporters and watchdog groups — have either been eliminated or have no remaining employees, according to a [recent review](https://www.oversight.gov/sites/default/files/documents/reports/2026-06/FY26%20F25DC0245%20%286.22.26%29v100_508_SECURED.pdf) by the agency’s inspector general.

A FOIA group within the Office of Elementary and Secondary Education, for example, had eight employees before President Donald Trump took charge in January 2025. After staff reductions, none remained, the report showed.

Those cuts have further jammed an already clogged system. A review by The 74 of publicly available [FOIA logs](https://www.ed.gov/about/ed-overview/required-notices/freedom-of-information-act-foia/foia-logs) shows that out of the more than 4,900 requests the Education Department has received since February 2025, only 3% have been granted in full or in part as of last week. The department doesn’t carry the largest backlog of requests, according to [a July 1 government-wide summary](https://www.justice.gov/oip/media/1450791/dl?inline). But its own annual [FOIA report](https://www.ed.gov/media/document/chief-foia-officer-report-2026-113323.pdf), released earlier this year, said it was unable to reduce a backlog that makes up over 86% of pending requests.

Officials blamed their lack of responsiveness on an increase in requests, the department’s downsizing and the 43-day government shutdown last year. Experts, including a former FOIA official, say the administration’s push to eliminate the department has especially hampered the public’s ability to understand the inner workings of the agency.

“The people who remain at the Department of Education are either not subject matter experts in the relevant issue or they’re massively overworked trying desperately to do the jobs of all of their departed coworkers,” said Jill Siegelbaum, who spent 15 years as the agency’s senior FOIA attorney.

With the department offloading key offices to other agencies, hunting down records could become even more difficult, she said. Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who will take responsibility for special education, has also [shuttered FOIA offices](https://www.politico.com/news/2025/04/03/kennedy-shutters-several-foia-offices-at-hhs-00268646).

“No one is going to have any idea what agency has which responsive records,” said Siegelbaum, now a partner with Sligo Law Group. “All of this adds up to a nightmare for FOIA requesters.”

Those include parents whose children aren’t receiving the services they’re entitled to, news outlets covering fast-moving stories in their local schools and student loan borrowers trying to make sense of what they owe after all the changes to [repayment programs](https://www.nbcnews.com/news/education/trump-big-beautiful-bill-student-loan-changes-rcna350126) in recent years.

The requests frequently become [outdated](https://www.poynter.org/reporting-editing/2026/public-records-requests-wait-times-too-long/).

In one example, a reporter from the New Bedford Light in Massachusetts asked for civil rights data from the local school district in 2021. The request is still pending, but the reporter has since moved on, according to the outlet’s executive editor.

In early 2023, Callie Oettinger, a special education advocate in Virginia, requested documents from the Office for Civil Rights related to its oversight of Fairfax County Public Schools during the pandemic. After an OCR investigation, [the district agreed](https://dcist.com/story/22/12/01/va-fairfax-schools-students-disabilities/) to make up for the services students with disabilities didn’t receive during school closures.

Oettinger wanted to ensure OCR was holding the district accountable, but two years later, she hasn’t received a response. She recently talked to a mom who is still trying to get services for her child as part of that agreement.

“We’re six years past COVID,” she said. “Kids needed help years ago.”

In March 2025, The 74 asked for submissions to the department’s short-lived tip line for examples of diversity, equity and inclusion activity in public schools. The department had to shut down the portal as the result of [litigation](https://www.the74million.org/article/education-dept-drops-appeal-in-case-challenging-anti-dei-letter/), but the request is still pending. In all, The 74 has submitted 20 requests since the current administration took office. Seventeen have not been processed and three cases were closed because they were duplicates. A separate request to the Office of Inspector General was fulfilled within four weeks.

FOIA wasn’t meant for “historical purposes,” said Jon Maier, senior litigation counsel at Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington. “It’s a public disclosure statute that’s designed to get information into the hands of the American public.”

The left-leaning watchdog group sued [multiple agencies](https://www.citizensforethics.org/legal-action/lawsuits/crew-sues-multiple-departments-for-records-on-doge/) seeking records from the Department of Government Efficiency, which oversaw widespread layoffs and cancelled contracts. Requesters can sue if they haven’t received a response in 20-30 days. The department’s current wait time for simple requests [is 172 days](https://www.foia.gov/agency-search.html?id=062dcf21-0ca7-4dee-8ea9-feb2de0d12ca&type=component), but many take much longer.

“That these preposterous delays are acceptable goes directly against the purpose of FOIA,” Maier said. The backlog is only likely to grow. “When the government isn’t telling you what it’s doing there will only be more FOIA requests.”

### **‘Ignoring FOIA officers’**

In addition to tallying the staff cuts, the inspector general’s office hoped to evaluate the agency’s ability to process requests. But the department refused to turn over all the information its internal watchdog requested and declined to “permit unrestricted access to its staff,” said Catherine Grant, a spokeswoman for that office. “We were unable to make definitive determinations in several areas, including major activities,” like FOIA requests.

Challenges finding affordable childcare have also worsened for some families this year as they face delays with receiving state subsidies reducing the […]
articles.full_body:
Title: Hawaii’s Childcare Shortage Is One of the Nation’s Worst

URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/zero2eight/hawaiis-childcare-shortage-is-one-of-the-nations-worst

Published Time: 2026-07-20T14:30:00-04:00

Markdown Content:
_Join our zero2eight Substack community for more discussion about the latest news in early care and education._[**Sign up today.**](https://zero2eight.substack.com/)

Nearly every child in Hawaiʻi lives in a community that lacks adequate licensed childcare, with access rates falling far behind the national average, according to recent reports from the Center for American Progress.

Challenges finding affordable childcare have also worsened for some families this year as they face delays with receiving state subsidies reducing the costs of preschool.

[The national reports](https://www.americanprogress.org/article/americas-licensed-child-care-deserts/)measure the percentage of kids in every state who live in childcare deserts — communities where at least three kids are vying for a single seat in a licensed program. These programs include options like public or private preschools, group childcare homes and Head Start, said Hailey Gibbs, associate director of early childhood policy at the Center for American Progress.

In ​​Hawaiʻi, 96% of kids live in these deserts — more than double the national average. Of these kids, 86% live in communities with no supply of licensed childcare providers at all, according to the Center for American Progress report.

The data highlights the need for greater investments in childcare programs, including more support for staff, Gibbs said.[Educator shortages](https://www.civilbeat.org/2024/06/staffing-shortages-persist-as-hawaiis-effort-to-expand-preschool-moves-forward/)mean that some programs that are already in high demand may need to cut back on the number of students they serve, she said.

“Provider financial viability is essential to standing up a childcare system that can actually meet demand,” she said. “You really need to be able to attract educators into the field.”

The study does not include unlicensed sources of care, such as grandparents, neighbors and[family and child interaction learning programs](https://www.civilbeat.org/2026/01/early-learning-sites-could-close-soon-in-hawaii-amid-federal-funding-chaos/), which allow kids to learn alongside their family members, said Malia Tsuchiya, early childhood policy and advocacy coordinator at Hawaiʻi Children Action’s Network.

Even taking these options into account, she said, families need more access to early learning programs. The state has aggressively expanded its public preschool programs in recent years, adding more than 1,800 seats in traditional public and charter schools since 2022. But the capacity of private childcare providers has fallen by roughly 950 seats in the same time period, according to a[2025 report](https://earlylearning.hawaii.gov/ourpages/auto/2025/12/27/35090178/08-31-25_Yamane-Arikawa-Cross_Act46ReportSubmissionforAugust2025_signed.pdf?rnd=1766823345090)from the Department of Human Services and Executive Office on Early Learning.

Hawaiʻi’s workforce of early educators shrank during the pandemic and never recovered, Tsuchiya said, leading some providers to close some classrooms and reduce their enrollment.

“We’re blessed to have multi-generational families and aunties, uncles, cousins, grandmas that are willing to step in and help supplement childcare,” Tsuchiya said. “But do they have a choice?”

### State Aid On Its Way?

For some families, securing childcare has only become harder to navigate as they face delays with receiving state tuition subsidies.

Operating under the Department of Human Services, Preschool Open Doors provides tuition subsidies for young children whose families make up to 500% of the federal poverty level. The maximum subsidy ranges from $1,200 to $1,500 a month.

Typically, the application window opens near the[start of the year](https://humanservices.hawaii.gov/dhs-preschool-open-doors-priority-application-period-opens-on-february-18-for-the-2025-2026-school-year/)to give the department time to process families’ applications before the payment period begins on July 1. This year, the application opened on[May 29](https://governor.hawaii.gov/newsroom/nr-preschool-open-doors-application-period-opens-for-2026-2027-school-year/), giving the department just over a month before families expected their subsidies to kick in. Parents need to reapply for the subsidy every year.

The applications opened later than usual because the Department of Human Services needed to update its administrative rules expanding Preschool Open Door’s age eligibility to 2-year-olds. The department was initially expected to expand the program to 2-year-olds back in January, but it instructed families to[hold off](https://www.civilbeat.org/2026/01/hawaii-preschool-aid-may-not-kick-in-this-school-year-after-all/)applying at the time to create more time for the rule change.

![Image 1](https://www.the74million.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/childcare-hawaii.jpg)

Hawaiʻi has some of the most widespread childcare deserts in the country, according to the Center for American Progress. (Screenshot/Center for American Progress)

As of last week, the program received over 6,100 applications and had processed 1,282. Staff process 10 to 15 applications a day, Joseph Campos II, director of the Hawaiʻi Department of Human Services, said in an emailed statement. The department is exploring strategies to reduce the backlog, he said, adding that wait times are longer than usual because of the high number of submissions the department received as soon as applications opened in May.

But the delayed release date has left families scrambling for answers.

Parent Christine Russo said she submitted an application for her twin daughters the morning of May 29, hoping an early submission would secure a quick response time from Preschool Open Doors. But more than a month later, she doesn’t know if her daughters qualify for the program — and the subsidy she was receiving through June has stopped.

Last year, Russo’s daughters qualified for Preschool Open Doors, meaning that she paid only $28 a month for their preschool tuition. But the program year ended on June 30, meaning that Russo is now covering the full costs of tuition for July — over $2,700 for both girls.

Russo said she doesn’t want to pull her daughters out of preschool, since it would mean losing her deposit and two spots that are in high demand. Russo lives in ʻEwa Beach, a community with a scarce licensed childcare supply, according to the[Center for American Progress](https://www.americanprogress.org/feature/child-care-deserts/).

Expecting to cover the full costs of preschool this month, Russo is worried what will happen if the tuition payments don’t kick by August. Earlier this week, she learned her application is being processed, but she’s still waiting to hear if her daughters qualify

fact_packs.headline: Trump’s Education Department Leaves Mountain of Public Records Requests Unaddressed
facts: Title: Trump’s Education Department Leaves Mountain of Public Records Requests Unaddressed
URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/article/trumps-education-department-leaves-mountain-of-public-records-requests-unaddressed

_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)
Even before massive staff cuts at the Department of Education, it could take years for officials to process a request under the Freedom of Information Act.

But now, some offices that handled the hundreds of inquiries that pour in each month — from parents, reporters and watchdog groups — have either been eliminated or have no remaining employees, according to a [recent review](https://www.oversight.gov/sites/default/files/documents/reports/2026-06/FY26%20F25DC0245%20%286.22.26%29v100_508_SECURED.pdf) by the agency’s inspector general.

A FOIA group within the Office of Elementary and Secondary Education, for example, had eight employees before President Donald Trump took charge in January 2025. After staff reductions, none remained, the report showed.

Those cuts have further jammed an already clogged system. A review by The 74 of publicly available [FOIA logs](https://www.ed.gov/about/ed-overview/required-notices/freedom-of-information-act-foia/foia-logs) shows that out of the more than 4,900 requests the Education Department has received since February 2025, only 3% have been granted in full or in part as of last week. The department doesn’t carry the largest backlog of requests, according to [a July 1 government-wide summary](https://www.justice.gov/oip/media/1450791/dl?inline). But its own annual [FOIA report](https://www.ed.gov/media/document/chief-foia-officer-report-2026-113323.pdf), released earlier this year, said it was unable to

Officials blamed their lack of responsiveness on an increase in requests, the department’s downsizing and the 43-day government shutdown last year. Experts, including a former FOIA official, say the administration’s push to eliminate the department has especially hampered the public’s ability to understand the inner workings of the agency.

“The people who remain at the Department of Education are either not subject matter experts in the relevant issue or they’re massively overworked trying desperately to do the jobs of all of their departed coworkers,” said Jill Siegelbaum, who spent 15 years as the agency’s senior FOIA attorney.

fact_packs.headline: Hawaii’s Childcare Shortage Is One of the Nation’s Worst
facts: Title: Hawaii’s Childcare Shortage Is One of the Nation’s Worst
URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/zero2eight/hawaiis-childcare-shortage-is-one-of-the-nations-worst
_Join our zero2eight Substack community for more discussion about the latest news in early care and education._[**Sign up today.**](https://zero2eight.substack.com/)

Nearly every child in Hawaiʻi lives in a community that lacks adequate licensed childcare, with access rates falling far behind the national average, according to recent reports from the Center for American Progress.
Challenges finding affordable childcare have also worsened for some families this year as they face delays with receiving state subsidies reducing the costs of preschool.

[The national reports](https://www.americanprogress.org/article/americas-licensed-child-care-deserts/)measure the percentage of kids in every state who live in childcare deserts — communities where at least three kids are vying for a single seat in a licensed program. These programs include options like public or private preschools, group childcare homes and Head Start, said Hailey Gibbs, associate director of early childhood policy at the Center for American Progress.

In ​​Hawaiʻi, 96% of kids live in these deserts — more than double the national average. Of these kids, 86% live in communities with no supply of licensed childcare providers at all, according to the Center for American Progress report.

The data highlights the need for greater investments in childcare programs, including more support for staff, Gibbs said.[Educator shortages](https://www.civilbeat.org/2024/06/staffing-shortages-persist-as-hawaiis-effort-to-expand-preschool-moves-forward/)mean that some programs that are already in high demand may need to cut back on the number of students they serve, she said.
“Provider financial viability is essential to standing up a childcare system that can actually meet demand,” she said. “You really need to be able to attract educators into the field.”

reading.title: 伊恩每日·教育 | 学校安防升级、中考爆冷、拔尖人才、美国教育部FOIA积压、夏威夷托育荒漠
reading.lead: 今天的教育新闻里，既有学校安防的物理升级，也有录取分数的意外波动，还有高校与企业的协同培养。同时，美国教育部的信息透明度和夏威夷的托育短缺，提醒我们教育公平的挑战无处不在。每一则新闻背后，都关乎孩子、家庭和社会的未来。

sections.title: 山西临汾两所学校再添安防新装备
sections.dek: 校园安全从人防到物防的升级，是投入也是信号。
sections.body: 新华网山西报道，山西临汾两所学校近期配备了新的安防装备。具体装备类型和数量未在新闻中详细说明，但这一动作延续了近年来校园安防升级的趋势。从监控摄像头到一键报警系统，再到防冲撞设施，各地学校正在将安全从“人防”向“物防+技防”转变。对于家长来说，这无疑是一颗定心丸。但需要警惕的是，硬件投入不能替代安全教育——孩子们需要知道如何正确使用这些设施，以及在紧急情况下如何保护自己。同时，安防设备的维护和更新也需要持续投入，避免“建而不用”或“用而不管”。
sections.takeaway: 校园安防升级是好事，但硬件之外，师生的安全意识和应急演练同样重要。

sections.title: 铁一越秀校区中考录取“爆冷”，学校回应来了
sections.dek: 名校录取线波动，是偶然还是新常态？
sections.body: 广州日报新花城报道，铁一中学越秀校区今年中考录取出现“爆冷”，分数线低于预期。学校已作出回应，但具体原因尚未明确。这种现象并非孤例——近年来，多地名校录取线出现波动，背后可能是考生志愿填报策略变化、招生计划调整或试题难度影响。对于家长而言，这提醒我们：盲目追逐“名校光环”存在风险，录取线每年都可能因小样本波动而失真。更理性的做法是关注学校的长期办学质量、师资稳定性和学生发展路径，而非单一年份的分数线。同时，志愿填报时应留足安全空间，避免“冲高”过度。
sections.takeaway: 中考录取线波动是正常现象，家长应理性看待，避免被单一数据绑架。

sections.title: 联合推动化学化工领域拔尖人才培养 校领导带队走访校友企业
sections.dek: 高校与企业的协同，是拔尖人才培养的新路径。
sections.body: 华南理工大学新闻网报道，校领导带队走访校友企业，旨在联合推动化学化工领域的拔尖人才培养。这种“校友+校企”模式并非新鲜事，但值得关注的是其具体落地方式：企业提供实践平台和行业洞察，高校提供学术资源和理论指导，双方共同设计课程和项目。对于学生来说，这意味着更多接触真实产业问题的机会，有助于避免“学用脱节”。但拔尖人才培养不应只服务于少数“精英”——如何将这种合作模式的经验辐射到更多普通学生，是高校需要思考的。此外，校友企业的参与动机也需要透明化，避免变成变相招聘或商业推广。
sections.takeaway: 校企合作培养拔尖人才值得鼓励，但需确保普惠性和透明度。

sections.title: Trump’s Education Department Leaves Mountain of Public Records Requests Unaddressed
sections.dek: 美国教育部信息透明度的倒退，影响每一个关心教育的人。
sections.body: 据The 74报道，美国教育部积压了大量公共记录请求（FOIA）。自2025年2月以来收到的4900多个请求中，仅有3%得到完全或部分满足。原因包括员工大幅裁减——例如小学和中学教育办公室的FOIA小组原有8人，现已无人。政府停摆和部门精简进一步加剧了问题。这直接影响了家长、记者和监察机构获取教育政策信息的能力。当公众无法了解教育部的运作，政策制定和资源分配就容易脱离监督。对于普通家庭来说，这意味着他们可能更难获知学校资金使用、课程改革依据等关键信息。
sections.takeaway: 信息透明是教育公平的基石，FOIA积压问题需要制度性解决。

sections.title: Hawaii’s Childcare Shortage Is One of the Nation’s Worst
sections.dek: 夏威夷96%的儿童生活在托育荒漠，早期教育的缺口有多大？
sections.body: 据The 74报道，美国进步中心报告显示，夏威夷96%的儿童生活在“托育荒漠”——即每个许可托育名额至少有3个孩子竞争。其中86%的儿童所在社区完全没有许可托育机构。这一比例是全美平均水平的两倍多。原因包括补贴发放延迟、教师短缺等。早期教育的缺失不仅影响儿童发展，也制约家庭尤其是母亲的就业。对于夏威夷这样的高生活成本地区，托育费用更是雪上加霜。解决之道在于增加公共投资、提高托育从业者待遇，并简化补贴申请流程。
sections.takeaway: 托育短缺是系统性难题，需要政府、社区和家庭共同破局。

reading.synthesis: 今天的五则新闻，从校园安防到中考录取，从校企合作到信息透明，再到托育短缺，看似分散，实则都指向同一个核心：教育系统的韧性。无论是硬件升级还是制度完善，最终目的都是为每个孩子提供更安全、更公平、更高质量的成长环境。作为家长和教育者，我们需要在关注个体选择的同时，也留意系统性的变化——因为个人的努力永远无法替代制度的支持。

podcast.title: 安防升级、中考爆冷、人才合作与全球困境：今天教育圈发生了什么？
podcast.description: 从山西学校的安防新装备，到广州铁一越秀校区中考录取的意外，再到华南理工与校友企业的人才合作，以及美国教育部信息不透明和夏威夷的托育危机——五个故事串联起教育安全、公平、合作与公共信任的深层议题。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 你好，我是伊恩。今天想从一件小事说起：山西临汾的两所学校，最近添置了安防新装备。听起来像是一条地方新闻，但如果你把目光拉远，会发现它和今天其他几条教育新闻——广州中考的意外、大学与企业的握手、美国教育部堆积如山的未处理信息申请、夏威夷几乎覆盖全州的托育荒漠——共享同一个核心问题：我们究竟在为什么样的教育环境买单？今天，我们就来拆解这五个事件，看看它们如何影响你——学生、家长、或者职场人。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 先看第一件事。山西临汾两所学校再添安防新装备，这是新华网山西今天下午报道的。报道中没有详细说明是什么装备，但指向了校园安全投入的持续加码。近年来，从防冲撞设施到一键报警系统，校园安防已经成为各地教育支出的常规项。这当然是好事，但我想请你思考一个更根本的问题：这些装备是真正提升了安全感，还是仅仅缓解了成年人的焦虑？

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 第二件事来自广州。铁一越秀校区中考录取“爆冷”，学校回应来了。根据广州日报新花城的报道，铁一越秀校区今年的中考录取分数线出现了意外波动，被一些媒体形容为“爆冷”。学校随后做出了回应。这背后可能涉及多种因素：招生计划调整、考生志愿填报的博弈、或者某个分数段的集中扎堆。对家长来说，这提醒我们：中考录取不是简单的分数比拼，而是一场信息战。你不仅要了解自己的成绩，还要读懂其他家庭的决策逻辑。

blocks.speaker: listener
blocks.text: 伊恩，铁一越秀的“爆冷”是不是意味着学校教学质量下降了？要不要避开这样的学校？

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 你的担心很自然。但根据现有事实，报道中只提到录取分数线出现波动，学校也做了回应，并没有任何信息指向教学质量问题。一次录取的意外，更多反映的是当年志愿填报的博弈结果，而不是学校本身的实力变化。如果你只看一年的分数线做决策，就像用一天的天气判断气候。建议你至少看三年数据，同时关注学校的师资稳定性、生源入口和出口的匹配度。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 第三件事是华南理工大学校领导带队走访校友企业，联合推动化学化工领域拔尖人才培养。这条新闻来自华南理工大学新闻网。它看起来是常规的校企合作，但放在当下就业压力大的背景下，它给出了一个方向：大学正在主动把人才培养的链条延伸到产业端。对学化学化工的学生来说，这意味着实习机会、课题方向可能更贴近实际需求。对职场人而言，这也是一种信号：终身学习不是空话，而是产业倒逼的结果。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 把视线转到美国。第四件事来自The 74的报道，标题是《特朗普的教育部留下大量公共记录请求未处理》。报道中提到，自2025年2月以来，教育部收到的超过4900份信息自由法（FOIA）请求中，只有3%被完全或部分批准。原因包括员工大幅裁减、政府停摆等。更具体的是，中小学教育办公室的一个FOIA小组原本有8名员工，到2026年6月时已无人在岗。这直接导致公众——包括家长、记者、监督组织——无法及时了解教育部的运作。当信息不透明时，教育政策的讨论就容易变成空中楼阁。

blocks.speaker: listener
blocks.text: 美国教育部的事离我们太远了，跟中国家长有什么关系？

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 你说的对，地理上很远。但我想请你从这个案例中提取一个通用的教训：当教育机构的信息不透明时，受影响最大的是普通家庭。无论是美国教育部积压的FOIA请求，还是国内某些学校招生信息的不对称，结果都一样——掌握信息的人占据优势。所以，不管你在哪里，培养自己获取和核实信息的能力，比追逐任何名校都重要。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 第五件事是夏威夷的托育短缺。根据The 74报道，美国进步中心的最新报告显示，夏威夷96%的儿童生活在“托育荒漠”中——社区里每三个孩子争一个托位。更严重的是，其中86%的孩子所在的社区完全没有持证托育机构。夏威夷并非个例，它是全美托育危机的一个缩影。这背后的原因包括资金不足、幼师薪酬低导致人员流失。对家长来说，这不仅是找不找得到托班的问题，更是“我能否安心工作”的生存问题。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 好，五个故事讲完了。现在我们把它们串起来。山西的安防装备、广州的中考波动、华工的校企合作、美国教育部的信息黑箱、夏威夷的托育荒漠——表面上看，它们是安全、升学、人才培养、信息公开、早期教育五个不同领域的事。但底层都指向同一个问题：教育系统的资源配置是否公平、透明、可持续？安防装备是硬件投入，但如果没有配套的心理支持和应急演练，它可能只是摆设。中考录取是信息博弈，如果数据不公开，家长就只能凭感觉下注。校企合作是好方向，但如果只盯着头部企业，普通学生怎么办？信息透明是公共信任的基础，一旦断裂，再好的政策也会被怀疑。托育是起点，如果起点就缺位，后续的教育公平就无从谈起。

blocks.speaker: listener
blocks.text: 伊恩，你说的这些我都懂，但作为普通家长，我能做什么？

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 这个问题问到了关键。我建议你做三件事：第一，建立自己的信息源。不要只看学校发的通知，多关注教育部门官网、权威媒体的报道，甚至学会看原始数据。第二，加入家长社群，但保持批判。社群里的信息往往是二手甚至三手的，你需要交叉验证。第三，把每一次教育选择都当作一次小型的项目研究——设定目标、收集信息、分析利弊、做出决策，然后复盘。这个过程本身就是在培养你和孩子的信息素养。

blocks.speaker: ian
blocks.text: 今天我们从山西的校园安防开始，穿越广州的中考现场，经过华工的校企合作实验室，跨过太平洋看到美国教育部的信息迷雾，最后停在夏威夷的托育荒漠。这些故事没有给出标准答案，但它们共同提醒我们：教育从来不只是学校的事，也不只是分数的事。它是安全、公平、透明、合作的复杂系统。作为个体，我们或许无法改变全局，但我们可以选择更清醒地参与其中。我是伊恩，感谢你的收听，我们明天见。
